questions.q: Which component inside the kubelet exposes container resource metrics to the Metrics Server?
questions.explanation: cAdvisor (Container Advisor) is embedded inside the kubelet and exposes CPU, memory, and other container resource metrics via /metrics/cadvisor. The Metrics Server scrapes this endpoint.

questions.q: What is the key limitation of the Metrics Server compared to Prometheus?
options: It stores metrics in-memory only — data is not persisted
It cannot be queried via kubectl
questions.explanation: Metrics Server is an in-memory, short-term store. It provides current resource usage for kubectl top commands but does not persist historical data. Prometheus stores time-series data persistently.

questions.q: Which command shows CPU and memory usage sorted by highest memory consumption?
questions.explanation: kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory sorts the output by memory usage, highest first. Similarly --sort-by=cpu sorts by CPU usage.

questions.q: The Metrics Server must be separately installed — it is not part of a default Kubernetes cluster.
questions.explanation: True. Metrics Server is not installed by default in most Kubernetes distributions (including kubeadm clusters). You must deploy it manually using its manifest or via a Helm chart.

questions.q: Which kubectl command shows resource usage broken down by individual containers within pods?
questions.explanation: kubectl top pods --containers shows a row per container within each pod, displaying each container's CPU and memory usage separately.
